summary: Gruber addresses the two most common questions about Apple's upcoming smaller iPad: whether it will have a retina display and how it will be priced relative to the iPod Touch. He argues convincingly that the smaller iPad will debut without a retina display, since Apple must choose trade-offs and the primary attributes will be thinness, weight, and price. On pricing, he dismisses concerns about undercutting the iPod Touch by explaining that gadget prices follow a U-shaped curve where miniature devices carry a premium. He also speculates on naming, favoring 'iPad Air' over 'iPad Mini' since the device is more about being thin and light than small.
key_points: Every new iOS device has debuted with a non-retina display and gone retina two or three years later, so a non-retina smaller iPad follows the pattern
When designing anything, you pick one or two primary attributes and compromise on everything else — the smaller iPad's priorities are thinness, weight, and price
Gadget prices follow a U-shaped curve: big is expensive, small is cheap, miniature is expensive — the iPod Touch is miniature, the smaller iPad sits in the middle
Apple has no problem with iPod Touch sales being cannibalized by a cheaper, bigger iPad because the customer still bought an iPad
The smaller iPad should be compared to competing tablets and the regular iPad, not to the iPod Touch
money_quotes: When designing anything, you pick one or two primary attributes and you compromise on everything else.
Retina displays are premium features; the new smaller iPad is not the premium model in the lineup.
It's true that smaller generally implies cheaper, but miniature carries a premium.
Gadget prices tend to follow a U-shaped curve: big is expensive, small is cheap, miniature is expensive.

If a customer walks into the store and sees a $249 smaller iPad and decides to buy that instead of a $299 iPod Touch simply because it's cheaper and bigger at the same time, that's still a win for Apple. The customer just bought an iPad.
Don't worry about comparing the price of the new smaller iPad to the iPod Touch. It's a different category.
"Mini" just doesn't feel like the right way to describe something that's remarkably thinner and lighter but not that much smaller than the regular iPad.
key_insight: Apple's smaller iPad will prioritize thinness, weight, and price over display quality, following the established pattern where every iOS device debuts non-retina and the primary design attributes dictate all other trade-offs.
